metadata.seed: Yeah, I just feel like you're the only one that can help me, you know, I mean, my boyfriend, like, things aren't going well with him. I thought he could help me, but I guess he can't. And I think he's running around on me and all that. So I was just like, well, let me reach out to doctor Grande and see if it can help me, because it seems like my last resort.

patient.query: Yeah, I just feel like you're the only one that can help me, you know, I mean, my boyfriend, like, things aren't going well with him. I thought he could help me, but I guess he can't. And I think he's running around on me and all that. So I was just like, well, let me reach out to doctor Grande and see if it can help me, because it seems like my last resort.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling really frustrated and a bit alone in this situation. I hear that you feel your boyfriend isn’t able to help and that you’re turning to me and Doctor Grande as a last resort. What do you think would help you feel more supported right now?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do feel pretty alone with all of this. I keep thinking that maybe if he could just understand where I'm coming from, it would help, but then it feels like I'm always in this loop of trying to explain things to him. I don’t really know what would help me feel supported… I just wish I didn’t feel like I had to carry all of this by myself.

neutral: Anxiety about not coping with parenthood (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight on your own and feeling stuck in a cycle of trying to explain yourself to him. That can feel exhausting and isolating. It’s understandable that you’d wish for more support and relief from carrying everything alone.

patient.query: Yeah, it is exhausting. Sometimes, I think I overthink everything I say to him, trying to make sure he gets it, you know? But then it feels like he just doesn’t get it, and I start wondering if I’m asking for too much or if I’m the one who’s not communicating well. It’s frustrating to feel like I’m constantly questioning myself.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure to make sure he understands everything you say. What signs do you notice that suggest he’s actually following along? Are there moments when he seems to get it or respond in a way that shows he’s on the same page? How might you test whether the communication is working or if something else is influencing the interaction?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: I guess there are moments when he nods or says something that makes me think he gets it, but then later he might say something that makes me feel like he didn’t really hear me at all. It leaves me feeling kind of... disconnected, like maybe I’m just not being clear enough. Testing it does sound interesting, but honestly, I’m scared of how it might go. What if I discover he really doesn’t care? It just feels like a lot to navigate.

entailment: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that your attempts to communicate might not be getting through, and that’s understandably unsettling. What kinds of cues or responses from him make you feel like he’s not really hearing you? And when you think about the possibility that he might not care, what would that look like for you, and how might you feel or act in that scenario?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: fear of saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: Yeah, when he changes the subject or doesn’t really engage with what I’m saying, it really stings. It’s like, am I even worth listening to? If I believed he didn’t care... I think I’d just feel really small and maybe even shut down. It’s hard to imagine trying to share anything with him after that, like what’s the point? It just makes me feel like I’m on shaky ground with everything, you know?

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re hurt when the conversation shifts and you feel like you might not be worth listening to. What signs or moments make you think he might not care about what you’re sharing? Could there be other reasons he changes the subject that don’t reflect his interest in you?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: I think sometimes when he changes the subject, it feels like he’s brushing off what I said, almost like it's too heavy for him or something. I mean… I get that maybe he just doesn’t know how to respond, but it still hurts. Deep down, I wonder if he’s just not really that into what I’m feeling, and that makes me feel really vulnerable and anxious. It’s confusing because I want to believe he cares, but those moments make it hard to hold onto that

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling hurt and vulnerable when he shifts the topic, and that’s understandable—those moments can feel like a dismissal of what matters to you. It’s natural to worry that he might not share your emotional depth, especially when you’re already anxious about how much control you have over your feelings. You’re not alone in feeling this way, and it’s okay to acknowledge that these experiences are weighing on you.

patient.query: Yeah, it feels like a real blow when he changes the subject like that, and it makes me question whether I’m just being too much. I get caught up in this cycle of feeling anxious about how much I’m sharing and if it’s worth it to even keep trying. I know I shouldn’t doubt myself so much, but it’s hard not to when it feels like he’s not entirely there with me. It just makes everything feel heavier, you know?

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: How do you know that you’re being too much? What signs or words from him make you feel he’s not fully present? Could there be other reasons he shifts the topic—maybe he’s just distracted or needs a break? What might happen if you tried to explore that possibility together?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: It really feels like a blow when he changes the subject. I can't help but wonder if I'm just being too much. I get anxious about how much I'm sharing and if it's worth it. I know I shouldn't doubt myself, but it's hard when it feels like he’s not fully there with me. It just makes everything feel heavier.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re hurt when the conversation shifts and that makes you question whether you’re sharing too much. Feeling anxious when you sense your partner isn’t fully present is understandable, and it’s normal to feel that weight. You’re not alone in this, and it’s okay to explore what that anxiety looks like for you.

patient.query: Yeah, it does hurt. I really want to connect, but the shifting feels like it just breaks that connection, you know? And then I second-guess everything—what I’m saying, how I’m saying it. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just overreacting, but it feels so real in the moment. I just want to feel heard and supported, but it’s like each time that happens, it gets a little harder to keep trying.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy sense of hurt and uncertainty whenever you try to reach out, and that can feel exhausting. It’s understandable that you’d worry about saying the wrong thing and second‑guess yourself, especially when you’re craving to be heard and supported. You’re not alone in feeling this way, and it’s okay to acknowledge how real and painful it feels right now.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: It does hurt. I really want to connect, but when he shifts the topic, it feels like that connection just breaks. I start to second-guess everything—what I’m saying, how I’m saying it. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just overreacting, but it feels so real in the moment. I just want to feel heard and supported, but it gets a little harder to keep trying each time that happens.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
